content:
12 min read
Just now

--

Okay, so picture this: you’re sitting there, maybe sipping your morning brew, and you imagine a world where you just poof your massive codebase from one language to another. Perfectly optimized, super safe, just ready to roll. In our wild and wonderful world of big language models-LLMs, as we call ‘em-and all that fancy code generation stuff, this dream feels, like, so close, doesn’t it? Especially when you hear whispers about “AI-powered refactoring” and migrations happening at warp speed. It’s a pretty sweet deal, right? This whole idea that an LLM can just effortlessly move a giant application-we’re talking 100,000 lines of existing TypeScript code, easy-peasy-to a high-performance powerhouse like Rust. But, hey, as someone who’s, uh, personally spent way too many hours untangling gnarly codebases and really getting down to the nitty-gritty of programming languages, I gotta tell ya, the reality? It’s way, way messier. 😅
Seriously, let’s just cut straight to it. While LLMs are, no doubt, incredible tools-like, mind-blowingly powerful-expecting them to actually “port” a massive TypeScript application over to Rust? That’s just a misunderstanding of what a real language migration even means, especially when you’re jumping to a systems language with Rust’s super unique vibes. Remember…
